mnemonics.title: Sparse Matrix Definition
mnemonics.content: Sparse Matrix: Mostly zeros, like an empty theater. Store only the seats with people (non-zero elements) for efficiency.

mnemonics.title: RCV - Row, Column, Value
mnemonics.content: Each triplet entry stores R-C-V: Row, Column, Value. Think of receiving (RCV) a package: the Row is the street, the Column is the house number, and the Value is what is inside the package.

mnemonics.title: Simple = Nested Loops, Fast = Pre-computed Positions
mnemonics.content: Simple transpose scans all elements for each column (nested loop = O(n*t), slow). Fast transpose pre-computes where each element should go using rowSize and rowStart arrays, then places them in one pass (O(n+t), fast).

mnemonics.title: Size then Start for Fast Transpose
mnemonics.content: The fast transpose uses two helper arrays: first compute SIZE (how many elements in each column), then compute START (where each column's elements begin in the result). Size counts, Start positions.

mnemonics.title: Mostly Zeros = Mostly Wasted
mnemonics.content: If a matrix is Mostly Zeros, storing it as a full 2D array means Mostly Wasted memory. The triplet form is like keeping only a guest list instead of an entire seating chart with thousands of empty seats.

mnemonics.title: Linked List for Dynamic Sparse Matrices
mnemonics.content: For frequently changing sparse matrices, use a linked list. Each node stores Row, Column, Value, and Next. Efficient for insertions and deletions.
